title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item Juliana. U-M created that electronic text in 1999 from the 1957 Oxford University Press reprint of EETS Original Series 51, not from either cached 1872 scan. The 1957 carrier reproduces Cockayne and Brock's 1872 Trübner edition, which prints one prose Life of Saint Juliana in parallel from British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII, ff. 56r–70r, and Bodleian MS Bodley 34, ff. 36v–52r, then a separate 228-line South English Legendary verse life from Bodleian MS Ashmole 43, ff. 25r–28r. U-M serializes the two prose streams and omits their modern-English renderings and the glossary. Complete public 1872 edition scans, the complete 580-canvas Ashmole 43 IIIF manuscript, a complete 2016 comparison edition, official Bodleian catalog TEI files, and the controlling U.S. copyright circular are cached locally; no current public complete image source was found for Bodley 34, and the British Library's historically reported full Royal surrogate is currently unavailable.
review_notes: Model two intellectual works, three encoded textual streams, and three physical manuscript witnesses. The Royal and Bodley prose streams are distinct recensions of one prose work; the Ashmole poem is a separate verse work.
The 1957 OUP reprint is the immediate U-M source. The cached Internet Archive PDFs are two original 1872 Trübner states and are supporting prior-edition facsimiles, not scans of the 1957 carrier.
Manuscript content, scholarly editing, digital encoding, modern photography, catalog metadata, and provider contract terms are separate rights layers.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for Juliana
entities.description: The checked-in XML source used by this repository, containing editorial front matter, two serialized prose witness streams, a five-line later Bodley addition, and a separate Ashmole verse life.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item Juliana
alternate_labels: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
entities.description: U-M Humanities Text Initiative electronic text created in 1999 by keyboarding the 1957 EETS reprint, then restructuring its parallel prose texts as successive divisions.
notes: The header credits keyboarding to ‘Chrisian’ and claims an error rate no greater than one error per 20,000 characters; no fuller identity is supplied.
The header's sole Latin language declaration is incomplete: the principal medieval texts are Middle English.

entities.label: Cockayne and Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, 1957 OUP reprint
entities.description: The immediate printed source identified by U-M. Its encoded title leaf names Oxford University Press in London, New York, and Toronto, and its verso carries Amen House and global-office matter absent from the cached 1872 Trübner states while retaining the inherited 1872 date.

bibliographic.citation: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D., with Renderings into Modern English, by O. Cockayne and Edmund Brock, edited by Oswald Cockayne (London: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1957), EETS Original Series 51; reprint of the 1872 edition.
bibliographic.title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 51
bibliographic.publisher: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society

notes: The evidence establishes a distinct 1957 reprint carrier but does not establish whether it was produced photographically, from standing type, from plates, or by another process.

entities.label: Cockayne and Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, 1872
entities.description: The original printed edition: Royal and Bodley prose recensions on facing pages with modern-English renderings beneath, followed by a five-line Bodley addition, a 228-line verse life from Ashmole 43, glossary, and corrections.

bibliographic.citation: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D., with Renderings into Modern English, by the Rev. O. Cockayne and Edmund Brock, edited by the Rev. Oswald Cockayne (London: N. Trübner for the Early English Text Society, 1872), EETS Original Series 51.
bibliographic.title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 51
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner for the Early English Text Society

notes: Cockayne's preface says the left-hand text was edited by Brock. The physical arrangement and headings identify this as the Royal text on even pages; Cockayne edited the Bodley stream and the collection generally.

The printed responsibility statement and contents assign the modern-English renderings to Cockayne and Brock: Brock edited and rendered the Royal stream, while Cockayne edited and rendered the Bodley stream. Those translations belong to this source edition but were expressly omitted from the U-M/General Corpus item.
The 1872 title's ‘Old English’ is historical terminology; current catalogs classify the witness texts as Middle English.

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII
entities.description: Composite parchment codex whose early-thirteenth-century part contains Katherine Group works; the prose Life of Saint Juliana occupies ff. 56r–70r.

date_statements.label: Relevant manuscript part
date_statements.value: First half of the thirteenth century

holding.notes: Life of Saint Juliana at ff. 56r–70r; consult in the British Library's London reading rooms.

physical_description.extent: ff. i + 97, plus unfoliated paper flyleaves; relevant early part ff. 1–70; Juliana ff. 56r–70r
notes: The relevant part is in Middle English associated with the West Midlands / AB-language manuscript group.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34
entities.description: Small parchment Katherine Group codex copied by one main scribe; the prose Life of Saint Juliana occupies ff. 36v–52r and loses text with a missing leaf between fols. 40 and 41.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: First half of the thirteenth century, perhaps c. 1240

holding.notes: Seinte Iuliene at ff. 36v–52r; five-line late-sixteenth-century verse paraphrase at fol. 52.

physical_description.extent: i + 81 + i folios, foliated i and 1–80; fol. 20b is an inserted slip; Juliana ff. 36v–52r
physical_description.format: One column, 25 long lines; one main early-textualis scribe
notes: The catalog identifies Middle English in the south-west Midlands AB language and suggests a possible Hereford origin with low certainty.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 43
entities.description: Large South English Legendary parchment codex; the separate 228-line verse Life of Saint Juliana occupies ff. 25r–28r.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fourteenth century

holding.notes: Life of Saint Juliana at ff. 25r–28r; Digital Bodleian canvases 63–69.

physical_description.format: One column, usually 36–47 lines; one small Anglicana hand
notes: The catalog localizes the consistent scribal orthography to Gloucestershire.

entities.label: British Library legacy full digital surrogate of Royal MS 17 A XXVII
entities.description: The current catalog preserves a historical statement that full digital coverage exists, but the current Digitised Content field is blank and the legacy manuscript viewer routes redirect to a generic recovery page.
entities.reproduction_format: Digital manuscript page images; exact current inventory unavailable
notes: Do not infer current downloadability or image completeness without recovering and auditing the actual image inventory.

entities.label: Facsimile of MS Bodley 34, EETS OS 247 (1960)
entities.description: Printed photographic facsimile of the complete manuscript with an introduction by N. R. Ker.

bibliographic.citation: Facsimile of MS. Bodley 34: St. Katherine, St. Margaret, St. Juliana, Hali Meiðhad, Sawles Warde, with an introduction by N. R. Ker (London: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1960), EETS Original Series 247.
bibliographic.title: Facsimile of MS. Bodley 34
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 247
bibliographic.publisher: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of MS Ashmole 43
entities.description: Complete 580-canvas IIIF Presentation facsimile of the manuscript, including covers, endleaves, adjacent works, and the Juliana portion at canvases 63–69.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest and JPEG service derivatives

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of the 1872 edition (elifladeofstjuli00cock)
entities.description: Complete public scan of an original 1872 Trübner issue, including EETS reports and advertisements around the edition.
entities.reproduction_format: 150-page PDF scan with OCR and scan metadata derivatives

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of the 1872 edition (StJuliana1230)
entities.description: Complete public scan of another original 1872 Trübner issue; Internet Archive metadata applies the Public Domain Mark 1.0 to this item.
entities.reproduction_format: 146-page PDF scan with OCR and scan metadata derivatives

entities.label: Huber and Robertson, The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), 2016
entities.description: Modern edited text and translation of the Bodley 34 Katherine Group, including the prose Juliana; used here as a comparison source, not as an upstream source of U-M's 1999 encoding.

bibliographic.citation: The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), edited and translated by Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson (Kalamazoo, MI: Medieval Institute Publications, 2016), Middle English Texts Series.
bibliographic.title: The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34)

entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A: Duration of Copyright
entities.description: Official current duration guidance used to separate the medieval works and 1872 edition from later editorial, encoding, and image layers.

scope.notes: Identifiers, title, structure, and contents agree; the exact transfer date and any repository-side normalization are not documented.

corrections from p. 91 integrated into the text

access.access_method: British Library Archives and Manuscripts parent and component records
Register for or renew a Reader Pass, obtain the required letter of introduction, and request the item for consultation in London
access.format: Original manuscript in a London reading room

access.access_method: The catalog historically reports full digital coverage, but the current record exposes no digitised-content link and legacy viewer URLs redirect to the generic digitised-manuscripts recovery page
access.format: Legacy digital manuscript images; currently inaccessible

access.access_method: Use the British Library online digitisation order form with shelfmark Royal MS 17 A XXVII, title, and folios 56r–70r; specify reference or reproduction quality and whether colour is required
access.format: Reference or reproduction-quality digital images, subject to item condition and available surrogates
notes: The service warns that original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned and that an existing surrogate may instead be supplied in black and white unless colour is requested.
Publication/reuse permission is separate; the service directs licensing questions to licensing@bl.uk.

access.access_method: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog page and official TEI source
access.format: Online HTML catalog and TEI XML source

access.access_method: Apply for a Bodleian Reader card with Group A access, state the research interest, and order the manuscript for consultation at the Weston Library; independent researchers are explicitly welcomed
access.format: Original manuscript in a Weston Library reading room

access.access_method: Complete the mediated-copying order form for reference-quality PDF scans; identify MS. Bodl. 34 and fols. 36v–52r, and ask first if complete-manuscript scanning or foliation is uncertain
access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans with basic shelfmark/folio metadata
notes: The current page states £1 per scan for non-Oxford users and an average turnaround of 10 working days, subject to legal, conservation, and other exceptions.
Images are supplied under Imaging Services terms; request written terms before repository publication or commercial print-on-demand reuse.

access.access_method: Consult the printed facsimile at the Folger Shakespeare Library; the catalog lists an open-stacks copy
Borrow through Internet Archive's controlled digital lending interface; only encrypted LCP/ACS derivatives are public, while the unencrypted PDF is private
notes: No access controls were bypassed and no controlled files were cached.

access.access_method: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog page and official TEI source
access.format: Online HTML catalog and TEI XML source
notes: The Juliana item has the correct title and locus but appears to duplicate the neighboring Julian the Hospitaller incipit and explicit; see the open question.

access.access_method: Digital Bodleian viewer and complete IIIF Presentation manifest

local_copies.label: Complete Bodleian MS Ashmole 43 IIIF facsimile bundle at 1600-pixel width

work_portion.label: South English Legendary Life of Saint Juliana
notes: Direct image inspection confirms that fol. 25r begins the Juliana life after the preceding legend and fol. 28r contains its conclusion before the next saint's life.
The complete bundle also preserves all other manuscript leaves, covers, endleaves, and adjacent works.
ZIP contains the provider manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 580 JPEG service derivatives requested at width 1600 pixels; these are not preservation masters.
Reused by hardlink from a previously verified byte-identical General Corpus Ashmole 43 bundle; Juliana worktree and main-cache inode/size/SHA-256 were audited as identical.

access.format: IIIF Presentation 2 and Image API JPEG derivatives

work_portion.label: The Liflade ant te Passiun of Seinte Juliene
notes: The complete 358-page PDF, not a Juliana-only excerpt, is cached.

The provider bytes are preserved unmodified. qpdf can open and process the file but returns exit 3 with recoverable warnings about many objects at offset 0; pdfinfo reports 358 pages and page/text extraction succeeds. Do not describe the file as qpdf-clean.

access.format: HTML digital edition and complete PDF

local_copies.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A (2026 retrieval)
notes: Hardlinked from a previously verified byte-identical General Corpus cache object and re-audited in both Juliana cache locations.
The provider bytes are preserved unmodified. qpdf can open the file but returns exit 3 for recoverable linearization-hint warnings; pdfinfo and text extraction succeed.

rights.component: The two underlying anonymous medieval works and their medieval witness readings, excluding modern editing, encoding, translation, and photography
rights.basis: The prose work was composed around the end of the twelfth century and the verse work is attested in a manuscript of 1300–1325, centuries before modern copyright terms.
notes: This does not authorize reuse of a modern edition, XML encoding, catalog prose, manuscript photograph, or provider interface.

rights.component: The 1872 edition's original editorial text, translations, arrangement, glossary, notes, and typography
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1872 and is far outside the maximum U.S. term for works first published before 1978.
notes: This status concerns the historical edition content, not necessarily modern scan files or provider terms.

rights.component: Any original 1957 publisher matter or alterations distinct from the inherited 1872 edition
rights.basis: The inherited 1872 content is public domain in the United States, but the extent and status of any new 1957 matter have not been established from a complete inspected copy.

rights.component: The U-M Juliana digital item as currently presented
rights.basis: The current item page says the materials are in the public domain and supplies the DPLA statement No Copyright – United States.
notes: This current provider statement conflicts in practical scope with the more restrictive availability paragraph embedded in the 1999 XML; both dated layers are retained.

rights.component: The 1999 machine-readable text under the availability statement embedded in its header
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The embedded statement permits individual copying for personal use, research, and teaching with the notice, and permits links including from for-profit editions, but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers.
notes: Because the current U-M page now states public domain/No Copyright – United States, ask U-M which statement governs redistribution of the XML encoding before relying on either alone.

rights.component: The checked-in XML artifact, including inherited U-M markup and repository-specific history
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: No repository-level license was found that independently resolves the conflict between U-M's current no-copyright statement and the restrictive availability paragraph embedded in the XML.

rights.component: Modern Digital Bodleian photographs and IIIF image derivatives of MS Ashmole 43
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide subject to the license and applicable law
rights.basis: The item-level IIIF attribution states Photo © Bodleian Libraries, University of Oxford and applies CC BY-NC 4.0; the Digital Bodleian terms limit use to noncommercial purposes except specified academic uses or separately licensed commercial use.
notes: The noncommercial license applies to the image files, not to the medieval text itself.
Commercial print-on-demand or a profit-oriented repository use requires separate permission unless it fits a specifically permitted academic-publication category.

rights.component: Historical British Library digital manuscript photographs
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The image inventory and item-level reuse statement are not currently accessible, so no image license or jurisdiction-specific public-domain assertion can be verified.

rights.component: Any reference or reproduction images newly supplied by British Library Digitisation Services
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The order page states that restrictions and image copyright may apply and directs reproduction users to obtain the necessary permissions and consult licensing staff.

rights.component: Any scans supplied by Bodleian mediated copying or Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The mediated-copying page supplies scans under Imaging Services Terms of Use and does not grant item-specific repository-publication or commercial-reuse permission.
notes: Ask for written permission before committing supplied scans or using them in a print-on-demand publication.

rights.component: The 1960 facsimile's photographs, N. R. Ker introduction, layout, and other modern contributions
rights.basis: The facsimile was published in 1960 and includes modern photography and scholarship; no open license was found and U.S. publication-formality/renewal status was not researched.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and OCR derivatives
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The cached item metadata contains no rights or license field. The photographed 1872 edition is public domain in the United States, but no broader claim is inferred for the modern files.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and OCR derivatives
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide as asserted by the provider, subject to applicable law
rights.basis: Internet Archive's item metadata supplies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 for this scan item.
notes: The Public Domain Mark is recorded as the provider's assertion, not as a warranty about every jurisdiction.

rights.component: The 2016 edited text, translation, introduction, notes, and downloadable PDF
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: METS says its digital editions are for individual use only and directs users to contact the Executive Director to reprint the digital text. It separately licenses XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, which does not automatically license the cached PDF or all edition content.

editorial_practices.description: U-M omits both modern-English prose renderings and the short glossary on printed pp. 89–90 while retaining the medieval texts, front matter, notes, and integrated corrections.
notes: The encoding therefore cannot substitute for the printed edition when comparing translations or glossary decisions.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Royal and Bodley prose recensions printed on facing pages
editorial_practices.representation: Two successive XML divisions, Royal first and Bodley second
editorial_practices.description: U-M transforms the source edition's parallel layout into a serial reading order without treating the recensions as one merged text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Cockayne's supplied title Juliana and manuscript labels
editorial_practices.representation: Editor-supplied titles retained; U-M adds bracketed manuscript names to distinguish the prose streams
editorial_practices.description: The electronic headings are editorial navigation, not diplomatic manuscript rubrics.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Words divided by printed page breaks or manuscript folio milestones
editorial_practices.representation: Joined search forms placed before the break while milestone integrity is retained and exceptional joins are listed in the header
editorial_practices.description: U-M normalizes nine explicitly listed split words for searching across page or folio boundaries.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Printed correction table and editor's corrected readings in notes
editorial_practices.representation: Corrected readings inserted into the text with the printed/manuscript form retained as sic
editorial_practices.description: U-M integrates the edition's terminal corrections and note-level corrections rather than reproducing the correction table as a separate appendix.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/Juliana.xml.
evidence.locator: HEADER; title page; DIV1 nodes Juliana:1–4; DIV2 node Juliana:3.1
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1999 U-M encoding and 1957 immediate source; preserves the OUP reprint title/verso; documents keyboarding, omissions, serialized witness layout, supplied headings, bad Bodley foliation, word joins, and integrated corrections; and encodes the two prose streams, later addition, and Ashmole verse life.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, item Juliana, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: Current item page confirms the title, editors, 1957 OUP publication, persistent identifier, contents, public-domain statement, and DPLA No Copyright – United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: CiNii Books, NCID BA2108971X, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, EETS OS 51 (1957).
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact 1957 Oxford University Press reprint, EETS Original Series 51, editors/responsibility statement, and library holdings discovery.

evidence.citation: Laurent Brun, ‘The life of saint Juliana,’ ARLIMA, record 10691.
evidence.summary: Distinguishes the one prose version in Royal and Bodley from the separate 228-line Ashmole verse version; confirms 1872 bibliographic data, printed page ranges, public scans, and the 1957 reprint.

evidence.citation: Thomas Oswald Cockayne and Edmund Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, EETS OS 51 (London: N. Trübner, 1872), two complete Internet Archive scan states.
evidence.locator: Title page; preface p. vii; prose texts pp. 2–80; Ashmole poem pp. 81–87; glossary pp. 89–90; corrections p. 91
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the original Trübner carrier, parallel Royal/Bodley layout, Brock's left-page editorship, modern-English renderings, later Bodley addition, 228-line Ashmole text, glossary, and correction table. Both cached PDFs are original 1872 states, not the 1957 OUP reprint.
notes: A second complete scan is cached at SHA-256 4c32fffe823da6ba8205866e278732c4e56e5d632f2a55135508aa3a14289b20.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item metadata for StJuliana1230.
evidence.summary: The provider metadata identifies the 1872 edition and applies Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 to this scan item.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 17 A XXVII.
evidence.summary: Provides the parent codex extent, dates, languages, origin, physical access conditions, and a historical ‘Full digital coverage available’ note while exposing no current Digitised Content link.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 17 A XXVII, ff. 1–70.
evidence.summary: Confirms the relevant part's 1200–1249 date, Middle English/West Midlands context, Juliana at ff. 56r–70r, and letter-of-introduction/physical-request conditions.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitised manuscripts and archives.
evidence.summary: States that some digitised manuscripts remain unavailable following the cyber-attack and directs users to the current Archives and Manuscripts catalog for available content.

evidence.citation: Direct checks of the historical British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII FullDisplay and Viewer routes.
evidence.summary: Both historical routes redirect to the generic digitised-manuscripts page; no manuscript image inventory or viewer is exposed as of the review date.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation services.
evidence.summary: Provides the online order route, required shelfmark/title/folio information, reference-versus-reproduction options, colour/surrogate caveats, payment requirement, and separate licensing warning.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS Bodley 34, official TEI source.
evidence.locator: msItem 3; additions; physical description; origin; bibliography
evidence.summary: Confirms Seinte Iuliene at ff. 36v–52r, the missing leaf, AB language, first-half-thirteenth-century South-West Midlands origin, possible Hereford association, 81-leaf structure, later addition, and 1960 full facsimile.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special collections mediated copying.
evidence.summary: Confirms that all users may request reference-quality PDF scans, currently £1 per scan for non-Oxford users, averaging 10 working days, subject to exceptions and Imaging Services terms.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Using special collections.
evidence.summary: Welcomes independent researchers and explains Group A Reader-card access and Weston Library consultation.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, Facsimile of MS. Bodley 34, 1960, catalog record 40715.
evidence.summary: Identifies the complete 1960 EETS OS 247 photographic facsimile, its contents and N. R. Ker introduction, and an onsite open-stacks copy.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive records for two controlled copies of Facsimile of MS Bodley 34.
evidence.summary: Item metadata marks both copies access restricted; unencrypted PDFs are private and only encrypted loan derivatives are exposed publicly.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS Ashmole 43, official TEI source.
evidence.locator: South English Legendary head; Juliana item ff. 25r–28r; physical description; origin
evidence.summary: Confirms the Juliana title and locus, Middle English language, 1300–1325 England origin, Gloucestershire dialect analysis, codex extent, and official Digital Bodleian object; its Juliana incipit and explicit appear duplicated from Julian the Hospitaller.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Ashmole 43, complete IIIF Presentation manifest.
evidence.summary: Supplies 580 labeled canvases for the complete manuscript, item metadata, catalog link, and item-level attribution stating Photo © Bodleian Libraries and CC BY-NC 4.0.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of cached MS Ashmole 43 IIIF inventory and Juliana boundary images.
evidence.summary: Inventory labels map canvases 63–69 to ff. 25r–28r. Image 63 shows the Juliana opening after the preceding text; image 69 contains the final eight Juliana lines followed by the next saint's life, confirming the exact work boundary inside the complete 580-canvas bundle.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: Explains the default CC BY-NC 4.0 image license, required attribution, permitted academic/noncommercial uses, commercial restrictions, and licensing contact; users must also check item-specific terms.

evidence.citation: Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson, eds. and trans., The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34) (Medieval Institute Publications, 2016).
evidence.locator: Edition landing; introduction; Juliana printed pp. 141–167 / PDF pages 142–168
evidence.summary: Modern Bodley 34 edition and translation; provides codex context, probable prose composition date, region and audience, prose-versus-verse analysis, Latin-source discussion, and a complete comparison text of Juliana.
notes: The cached PDF is readable and text-extractable but qpdf reports recoverable object-offset warnings; provider bytes were not repaired or altered.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright & Citation.
evidence.summary: States that digital editions are for individual use and reprinting requires contact with the Executive Director; separately licenses XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and says print rights follow the edition's own guidelines.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.summary: Official U.S. duration framework used to identify the 1872 edition as public domain while avoiding unsupported conclusions about later reprint matter, modern editions, encodings, and photographs.

open_questions.question: What complete physical or digital copy was U-M's exemplar of the 1957 OUP reprint, and how exactly was that reprint produced from the 1872 edition?
next_steps: Locate and inspect a complete 1957 copy from a holding identified by CiNii or request U-M production documentation.
Compare typographic accidents, title/verso matter, pagination, advertisements, and correction state against both cached 1872 copies.
Do not describe the 1957 reprint as a photographic or plate facsimile without direct evidence.

open_questions.question: When will the historically reported full digital coverage of Royal MS 17 A XXVII be restored, and what is its exact image inventory and reuse statement?
next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether the legacy full surrogate remains internally available and whether a new viewer/IIIF object is planned.
If restored, download and audit the complete codex rather than only ff. 56r–70r, then map the Juliana portion within it.
Until then, use the physical-request or paid digitisation-order routes.

open_questions.question: Has Bodley 34 been fully digitized in a current public image service apart from the 1960 printed facsimile and controlled loan copies?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether an unindexed or staff-only whole-codex digital surrogate exists.
Request reference scans of ff. 36v–52r or, if feasible, the complete codex, together with written reuse terms.
Use the 1960 printed facsimile onsite or through lawful controlled lending for layout comparison.

open_questions.question: Will the Bodleian correct the Ashmole 43 Juliana item's incipit and explicit, which currently duplicate the neighboring Life of Julian the Hospitaller?
next_steps: Report the discrepancy with the exact item locus ff. 25r–28r and direct IIIF boundary evidence.
Continue using the facsimile, U-M text, 1872 edition, and ARLIMA incipit/explicit for identification until corrected.

open_questions.question: Which U-M statement governs redistribution of the Juliana XML: the current public-domain/No Copyright – United States item statement or the 1999 embedded ban on redistribution by commercial and nonprofit content providers?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for a written interpretation specific to downloading, modifying, and redistributing the XML.
Keep the public-domain underlying texts and 1872 edition separate from possible terms on U-M's encoding.
Do not infer that permission to link from for-profit works is permission to republish the XML.

open_questions.question: What is the correct reading of the signature following the late-sixteenth-century five-line addition at Bodley 34 fol. 52?
next_steps: Inspect a high-resolution image of fol. 52 and compare Cockayne's uncertain ‘A Maidwot (?)’ with the current catalog's ‘Quoth Maidwell.’
Do not assign a named author until the hand and wording are resolved.
